docs.title: OM-Files Python & Rust Package
docs.text:
Warning

This package is under active development and may change without notice.

Welcome to the documentation for the omfiles Python package, an interface for reading and writing .om files.

The OM file format is designed for efficient storage and access of multi-dimensional scientific data, supporting hierarchical structures, chunked arrays, and compression. The Python bindings are powered by PyO3, leveraging the rust-omfiles package.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Read and write .om files from Python and Rust.
  • Hierarchical data model: groups, datasets, and attributes.
  • Chunked and compressed arrays for efficient storage.
  • NumPy-style indexing and async access in Python.
  • Context manager support for safe resource handling.
  • Integration with fsspec for remote file access.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install the Python package (requires Python 3.8+):

pip install omfiles

Or build from source (see GitHub repository for details):

git clone https://github.com/open-meteo/python-omfiles.git
cd omfiles
pip install .

docs.title: Reading an OM file
docs.text:
from omfiles import OmFilePyReader

with OmFilePyReader.from_path("test_file.om") as reader:
    arr = reader[0:2, 0:100, ...]
    print(arr.shape)

docs.title: Writing an OM file
docs.text:
import numpy as np
from omfiles import OmFilePyWriter

# Create sample data
data = np.random.rand(100, 100).astype(np.float32)

# Initialize writer
writer = OmFilePyWriter("simple.om")

# Write array with compression
variable = writer.write_array(
    data,
    chunks=[50, 50],
    scale_factor=1.0,
    add_offset=0.0,
    compression="pfor_delta_2d",
    name="data"
)

# Finalize the file. This writes the trailer and flushes the buffers.
writer.close(variable)

The API reference is autogenerated directly from docstrings in the source code using mkdocstrings.

omfiles provides access to the following things:

docs.title: omfiles.OmFilePyReader
docs.text:
An OmFilePyReader class for reading .om files.

A reader object can have an arbitrary number of child readers, each representing a multidimensional variable or a scalar variable (an attribute). Thus, this class implements a tree-like structure for multi-dimensional data access.

Variables in OM-Files do not have named dimensions! That means you have to know what the dimensions represent in advance or you need to explicitly encode them as some kind of attribute.

Most likely we will adopt the xarray convention which is implemented for zarr which requires multi-dimensional variables to have an attribute called _ARRAY_DIMENSIONS that contains a list of dimension names. These dimension names should be encoded somewhere in the .om file hierarchy as attributes.

Therefore, it might be useful to differentiate in some way between hdf5-like groups and datasets/n-dim arrays in an om-file.

Group: Can contain datasets/arrays, attributes, and other groups. Dataset: Data-array, might have associated attributes. Attribute: A named data value associated with a group or dataset.

docs.title: close() method descriptor
docs.text:
Close the reader and release resources.

This method releases all resources associated with the reader. After closing, any operation on the reader will raise a ValueError.

It is safe to call this method multiple times.

docs.title: omfiles.OmFilePyReaderAsync
docs.text:
A reader for OM files with async access.

Provides asynchronous access to multi-dimensional array data stored in OM files. Supports reading from local files via memory mapping or from remote files through fsspec compatibility.

Methods:

Name Description close

Close the reader and release any resources.

from_fsspec

Create a new async reader from an fsspec fs object.

from_path

Create a new async reader from a local file path.

read_concurrent

Read data from the array concurrently based on specified ranges.

Attributes:

Name Type Description chunks tuple

The chunk shape of the variable.

shape tuple

The shape of the variable.

The OmVariable that serves as the root/entry point of the file hierarchy. All other variables should be accessible through this root variable.

None on success.

docs.title: write_array(data, chunks, scale_factor=1.0, add_offset=0.0, compression='pfor_delta_2d', name='data', children=[]) method descriptor
docs.text:
Write a numpy array to the .om file with specified chunking and scaling parameters.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default NDArray[Any]

Input array to be written. Supported dtypes are: float32, float64, int8, uint8, int16, uint16, int32, uint32, int64, uint64,

required Sequence[int]

Chunk sizes for each dimension of the array

required Optional[float]

Scale factor for data compression (default: 1.0)

1.0 Optional[float]

Offset value for data compression (default: 0.0)

0.0 Optional[str]

Compression algorithm to use (default: "pfor_delta_2d") Supported values: "pfor_delta_2d", "fpx_xor_2d", "pfor_delta_2d_int16", "pfor_delta_2d_int16_logarithmic"

'pfor_delta_2d' Optional[str]

Name of the variable to be written (default: "data")

'data' Optional[Sequence[OmVariable]]

List of child variables (default: [])

[]

Returns:

Type Description OmVariable

OmVariable representing the written group in the file structure

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If the data type is unsupported or if parameters are invalid
